text:
When servers lie

I've been to this ramen place probably a dozen times, at least 3 of which were since I've been vegan. In their defense, the vegan option is labeled as "veggie ramen", but I asked them every time to confirm that it was vegan and they always said it was. Today, after ordering the garlic edamame (was previously told it was vegan) I told the server it was great, and she said "oh, I know, butter and garlic go well together, right?!" And I just disintegrated. Mistakes happen, but this pissed me off, and now it makes me think that there's likely butter in the ramen too (previously thought the oil slick on top was garlic oil). Also, they sell a hoodie with a picture of a gorgeous pig, and underneath it says "I am chashu" which is the grossest sentiment for such a beautiful animal. Ok, that's it for me today. I'm grateful for this community. Thanks.
label: experience

Whilst you both don’t agree on your dietary views for various reasons, why can’t you both just come together and appreciate the similarities you do have and leave the differences aside?

As a strict carnivore I know how much effort and determination it is to eat a certain way when you have marketing and advertisement and social conventions working against you day and night. And that experience is no different to the vegan way of eating, or anyone else following a certain way of eating. 

If your friend is being bullish because they believe one thing and you don’t agree/like it then agree to not discuss that side of it. If you don’t appreciate her way of eating for the ethical side of it, again move past it and focus on the positives of your friendship. 

If either of you can’t see past your own personal views for the sake of what was a seemingly solid friendship then the friendship would never have been that strong to begin with.

text:
What do I do with non vegan food my caretaker bought specifically for me because he thought it was vegan?

I can't stand upright for long enough to cook for myself, so my father cooks and buys food for me. I went vegan recently\*, and we're still figuring out what foods to buy in order to create a standard Publix grocery list.

However, my dad occasionally buys something for me that includes milk way down on its ingredients list, thinking that it's vegan. As a new vegan, I really want to avoid breaking my diet, since I worry that allowing any lapse might snowball into a larger diet-break.

But, my family is getting sick of all my niche diet restrictions, and I'm worried that I might "break the camel's back," so to speak, at any moment. My dad is really going out of his way to find new vegan recipes and snacks for me, and I want to adequately show appreciation for his efforts and avoid complaining too much.

We agreed that we'd never buy the snack again, but that I'd eat it since it's already here. I feel guilty though, and I'm really worried that making any dietary exceptions while I'm in the throes of establishing new vegan boundaries can snowball into allowing even more harmful "exceptions" in the future.

What do I do with these snacks? Since snacks last forever, should I just wait until I feel more secure in my diet to eat them?  


\*I thought the reason I cite for becoming vegan might tickle people.

Whenever someone asks, I say, "The experience of being sick has showed me just how excruciating it is to live in a failing body. When I think about all the animals who have been bred to have broken bodies...I don't want anyone to feel this sort of suffering." Even militant carnists usually don't complain too much when I give this as my justification for becoming vegan.
label: experience

text:
I want to become vegan to protect my moral integrity

Hello, everyone. I wanted to share my reasons for becoming vegan. Please feel free to share your feedback. Yesterday and today I had a desire for eggs, then I felt bad because it went against my beliefs in bodily autonomy and consent. However, I still had eggs as a way to shut off the guilt. I didn’t feel very satisfied afterward. It made me examine my understanding of human nature. For me, I believe there are two sides of me: my conscience and my instincts. My instincts prioritize short term pleasure that takes no effort, so it will seek out cheap sources of dopamine like sugar. The part of me that felt bad about the eggs was my conscience and my instinctual side tried to shut off the guilt. I didn’t eat eggs for nourishment necessarily. I ate it for sensory pleasure and it primed my dopamine system to eat sugar. This constant dopamine stimulation from the dopamine priming from the animal product and the dopamine release from the sugar dulled my ability to care about anything. As a consequence, morals didn’t matter as much to me. I wouldn’t say I was becoming a bad person in the sense that I was actively harming people physically or emotionally. I was becoming an uncaring and less empathetic person. 

I feel like animal products and things like sugar, palm oil, and cacao are unethical, because I’ve felt the harm these things had on my spiritual and neurobiological health. It made me lifeless, unmotivated, and irritable. I was led to believe you needed these things to have a happy and balanced life. I was told moderation is key for optimal happiness. I kept buying into this narrative even though I felt guilty and it was bad for my health. This narrative made me put aside my conscience when my conscience is my highest form of wisdom. But of course I couldn’t moderate food that was engineered to derive the most sensory pleasure, and I kept consuming these unethical food products to chase after a fake happiness created by these recreational edible substances that trigger a dopamine release. It’s not food to me anymore. 

So the point I’m trying to make is that animal products and other unethically sourced food items made me put aside my ethics, and as a result nothing else mattered, including my physical health. The only way to be truly happy moving forward is to have my actions and beliefs be in spiritual alignment. I need to be a vegan to protect my moral integrity and humanity. I can’t be enslaved to my instincts that only wants to chase after short term pleasure (i.e. animal products, sugar, cacao, palm oil). I truly care about animal welfare and workers’ rights. Also, I wanna clarify that I’m not fighting my instincts. I want my instincts to be realigned with my conscience. I know humans are naturally predisposed to violence, greed, and pride, so I only want to bring out the best aspects of humanity in me. To me, being a vegan is a form of moral cultivating and a way to heal my soul. Anyway, I hope I didn’t come across as sanctimonious. I’ve been very unsatisfied with my life and I’m pretty sure this is why.
label: experience

text:
Why I Believe Veganism Should Also Include Minimalism and Choosing to Stay Childfree

I’ve noticed that many people call themselves vegan simply because they eat a plant-based diet. But to me, veganism is much deeper than just avoiding animal products in food.

At its core, veganism is about reducing suffering — especially the suffering of animals.

As humans, it's nearly impossible to avoid causing *any* harm. We need homes to live in, clothes to wear, transportation, mobile phones, electricity — all of which consume natural resources and indirectly cause environmental damage. Mining for metals and fossil fuels, deforestation for infrastructure or agriculture, and water and chemical usage for manufacturing — all of these things contribute to the destruction of ecosystems and animal habitats.

For example, if someone eats a plant-based diet but lives in a massive villa, drives five cars, and consumes excessively — can we really call that aligned with the spirit of veganism? That villa may have been built by clearing forest land. The furniture might be made of wood. The cars, even electric ones, require mining and energy that harm the environment.

The same goes for bringing new children into the world. I know this is a sensitive topic, but it’s worth considering: having a child means more consumption — paper, clothes, furniture, toys, energy, and eventually another house, another car, more electronics. All of this adds to environmental strain and indirectly contributes to animal suffering.

Of course, we can't reach *zero* suffering. We’re born into a world where consumption is inevitable. But we can **minimize** it. We can choose to live more simply, consume less, and be mindful of the consequences of our actions — beyond just our diet.

These are just my personal thoughts. I’d love to hear what others in the community think about this broader view of veganism.
label: arguments

Red Pills, Raw Meat: The Manosphere’s Influence on Men’s Health and the Environment

"A new report from environmental action nonprofit Hubbub, titled “High Steaks: How Young Men are Beefing Up Their Diets,” notes that young men in the UK between ages 16 and 24 are three times more likely than the general population to have increased their meat intake in the last year. 

One key reason, it suggests, is the growing influence of online influencers—aligned with the ideals of the manosphere—who push the belief that eating meat is masculine and that meat consumption equals strength and fitness. This can be seen in the uptick of [raw meat](https://vegnews.com/experts-warn-against-tiktok-raw-meat-diet) and [carnivore diet](https://vegnews.com/tiktok-carnivore-diet-risky) videos, many with millions of views, on TikTok and Instagram. 

It’s not just a problem in the UK. In the US, 2024 research from the Pew Research Center found that one-fifth of young American adults are increasingly getting their news from male, right-wing social media influencers."

Fair play, indeed taste is subjective, though personally I'm not a huge fan of the cathedral city as I find it TOO much like real cheese. Has more of that mature/farmyard aftertaste than most (standard supermarket) dairy cheddars, and that just tastes a bit too much like animal for my liking.

Now obviously if you're gonna compare a proper decent cheese to substitutes, they won't hold up, but that's like comparing the actual flesh of a dead animal to a sub that is trying to mimic it. It'll never accurately recreate it, it'll never be a 1 for 1 swap in flavour, texture, etc, and ultimately, I think if someone is looking for something that is an exact recreation of non-vegan goods, they will perpetually be disappointed, so better just to enjoy things for what they are, rather than comparing against something that they will never be.

text:
Attended a conference in Paris which promised "100% vegetarian menu"

https://preview.redd.it/fco4jmxa871f1.png?width=878&format=png&auto=webp&s=f26779897b6ed8b742621bd4bc36ea8d51625a6c

I was at a conference in Paris this week which promised in the registration email that the menu will be 100% vegetarian to meet their sustainability goals. I was excited to finally go to a conference where hopefully I won't starve (hopefully because I know French food is heavy on butter and cheese). But in reality, not only the menu was not 100% vegetarian, there were very few vegetarian options and almost no vegan options. Plus, none of the food items were marked vegan/vegetarian, and the caterers didn't speak English so I had great difficulty in knowing what's safe for me to eat. After surviving the first day on orange juice, I just decided to bring my own food from the second day onward.   


So much for sustainability. You can't control how people travel to the conference, and for most people, flying is the only option. But menu is in your control.

text: I'm not sure how it is in the so-called developing countries or the ones with natural huge grassland areas, but at least in our case, the grasslands are managed and treated as crops basically. Because when you harvest hay, you remove nutrients = deplete soil. Without the natural cycle of herbivores grazing, defecating, dying, and decomposing, the nutrients have to be returned = that's when the fertilisers come in. The same applies to grazed pastures cause in the long term, we harvest the cattle = again, nutrients are being transported away from the soil. And then you need a certain nutritional value of the forage. Thus, treatment is needed in case some undesirable species take over, wild boars destroy parts of the vegetation, etc.

text:
Please, help protect horses in the US from the cruel practice of soring

So last night I was reading about obscure and cruel practices in animal husbandry to stay updated, and among the ones that we are all already very familiar with (like docking, branding, debeaking, etc.), I learned about the practice of soring. I am sharing this here in case someone would like to learn about other practices they can act on or protest against that might fly under the radar.

# What you can do:

Many animal rights, horse welfare and rescue organizations are urging US citizens to send a message to the USDA to finally put an end to this end cruel practice and to stop delaying the implementation of the PAST Act (that was meant to be implemented THIS YEAR!!)

The Humane World for Animals has created a page on their website to facilitate this process if you wish to contribute with your voice and help: [https://humaneaction.org/action-center/protect-horses-cruel-practice-soring](https://humaneaction.org/action-center/protect-horses-cruel-practice-soring)

# About soring:

Soring gained popularity during the 1950s, and it consists of deliberately inflicting pain on horses' hooves and legs through the use of caustic chemicals that burn their flesh, heavy stacked shoes and chains or rollers around their forelegs that repeatedly hit the wounds or burns in their pasterns as they walk (among many other horrifying "training techniques"), creating an exaggerated gait highly coveted at "Big Lick" shows.

These abuses are supposed to be illegal under the Horse Protection Act of 1970 but they are still practiced thanks to the usual corrupt self-policing entities. This has been known for decades.

At the start of this year, there was hope that a long-overdue rule to update the Horse Protection Act regulations was going to be implemented, but the USDA has delayed its implementation until February, 2026, which opens a door to further alterations.

# Documentation and undercover investigations:

It's common to hear that this abuse is "a thing of the past", or that horses are happy and loved, well taken care of, and that they enjoy performing at these ridiculous events, but this couldn't be further from the truth. The Horse Plus Humane Society in Tennessee has been documenting and reporting the ongoing abuse, not only visible in the horses they rescue, but also by protesting during local "Big Lick Shows". Please do not watch these videos if animal suffering is too overwhelming for you right now, but I will link some examples below in case they are useful to someone as resources:

An snipped of the undercover investigation conducted by the Humane World for Animals: [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HYh0nD44HY8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HYh0nD44HY8)

A video of Serenity, a young victim of the Big Lick industry that was recently rescued by the Horse Plus Humane Society: [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=llME8VCSLCw](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=llME8VCSLCw)

Both youtube channels have more videos about this topic, and the Humane World for Animals has more articles about their investigations and updates news about soring and the PAST Act on their website.

Thank you for reading!

text:
I found many ways to make big human population vegan quickly!

I spent some time thinking deeply about how to encourage more people to go vegan quickly, and I came up with several strategies that could work quickly and effectively for large human population. Here they are:

1. Instead of men focus on women – If more women go vegan, they might prefer vegan partners and raise vegan kids. That could naturally grow the movement.

2. Give animal lovers a pet – People who say “I love animals” might really change once they bond with one. Giving someone a rabbit or herbivores pet might help them make the "connection" emotionally. This can end speciesism. Once they bond with it, they might start questioning eating animals next time you talk to them.

3. "Vegan-ish" is okay – Instead of pushing full veganism right away, we can encourage people to eat less meat. I read that about 80% of global meat production is for human food consumption, so small changes in eating habits can make a big difference.

4. Images on packaging – Push governments to make companies show how animals are actually treated or killed on meat/dairy packaging, instead of putting cute cartoons. That shock factor might wake people up.

5. Motivational packaging – Vegan brands should put inspiring quotes on their packaging to remind buyers they're doing something good and better than non vegans. (Humans like to gain respect). It also helps people feel proud and less likely to go back to eating animals because of repeated reminders.

6. Religion – Since a lot of people follow some religion in the world, spreading more compassionate interpretations of religious texts could help.

7. Government bans? – A top-down move like banning meat sounds strong, but I’m not sure if it would work or just cause backlash. What do you think about that? There are always some people who feel offended but such steps must be taken nonetheless.

8. Teach it in schools – Kids should learn about animal cruelty and factory farming in school. Show them real videos, share facts plant the seed early.

9. Promote Minimalism: Many people are too busy to reflect on their lives. Promoting minimalism can help them pause, reduce desires, and reconsider how societal conditioning has influenced their choices including food.

10. Free Vegan Samples: Serve tasty vegan food outside non-vegan restaurants with a big banner like “Free Vegan Food: No Animals Were tortured.” Let people know what they’re eating is vegan and delicious. Make sure your stand faces the restaurant.

11. Wealthy vegans could support homeless or struggling individuals by offering food and shelter in exchange for helping with vegan activism. This creates mutual benefit and spreads the message.

So yeah, those are my ideas. Took me a while to come up with them. I know some might be a little out there, but what do you think? Any of these stand out to you?

text:
Horn Cutting is Necessary

I recently saw a post saying its unnecessary to cut the horns off cattle. As someone has been working on a dairy farm for over a decade I will say horn cutting is entirely necessary both for the cattle’s safety and those who care for them. If left unattended they can injure other livestock or the farmer. Usually we remove them at 3-4 months old, the best age to do so, where we cauterize the area around the horn and simply snip it off with a pair of cutters. These are usually similar to what is used for large branches. It’s effective and takes about 5-10 minutes per cow depending on the time it takes to secure them in place. After the procedure they feel no pain and go on right back to the herd. It’s not uncommon to dehorn a calf and the next day they are raring for you to give them a bottle as if nothing happened so they are just fine.

text:
Lol finaly a post that has nothing to do with ethics 😂. Ok I got this.... first and for most, you should research. Im sure there are good tutorials online that help break down menus like at MC Donald's or subway. Also see what restaurants have a vegan selection or substitutes. If non then dont you worry. 

DISCLAMER: AS A NON VEGAN RESTAURANT YOU RUN THE RISK OF CROSS CONTAMINATION DUE TO SHARED UTENCILS. (Also some restaurants are gross af 🫣)

So i could break this down in many ways. But best practice is again, research. Do your due diligence. 

1st) know what not to eat
2nd) pick a style, personly I love mexican or asian (doesn't matter but they use more vegetables)
2.5) plan ahead (if you are inviting, check out places and call ahead to see if they can accommodate)
3rd)once you picked a spot, dont be afraid to ask the servers. 
4th) enjoy meals. 

Now to go more in depth. You should learn to cook 😅 it helps. Within my first year i was nieve and asumed alot of things were vegan, just to find out the wernt. Now I know eating out is good for socializing and bonding but ive grown to dislike it, unless its a full fledge vegan restaurant. Now thats not to say I dont go out from time to time. Reasons are simple, either the non veg eaters dont wanna take the risk of eaten in front of me or they feel bad if I dont eat (some times im just not hungry but enjoy the company so I will always offer to pay for meals) or I dont wanna get sick when the meal was contaminated while being prepared. So again a fact you gotta face is, foods are being prepared on  a shared surface, so you may end up getting sick. 

Now back to the whole slection. I prefer Mexican food, simple right? Well it can get tricky (this is where knowing how to cook and asking servers questions come into play) see I usually get a bean tostada or just a bowl of beans and rice maybe some avocado slices as well. Simple right? Well depending on the place they may add lard to the re-frided beans or throw pork parts in the bean pot for frijoles de la joya, or Mexican rice traditionaly is made with chicken stock. Specificaly consume de pollo, and if you dont speak spanish or specify, the may forget and say that there isn't animal products. Shoot some times i end up with a speck of cheese as well 😮‍💨 cant blame them because honestly its probably muscle memory for them.

Same goes for Asian food, sauces can contain fish, noodles can contain eggs. Stir-Fried may be done on a dirty flat top that they just got done cooking meats. I make due with miso soups. Generally are vegan, but doesn't hurt to ask on the ingredients. 

Other items as well, Beyond burgers, probably gonna be cooked on the same grill as a regular Burger, Veggies typicly are "blanched" meaning the are pre boiled to soften (cuts cooking time in half) no real issue unless its "steamed". Then the may get tossed into a pot of boiling water, that more than likely has been shared with noodles, shrimp, and anything else that needs a quick boil. And any thing deep fried is cooked with the same oil they made those amazing chiki nuggies with. (Except for 5 guys, i believe they just cook fries and thats it) Lol not trying to discourage you but these are the facts. To be honest I have a tiny back ground in cooking so I have no issue dismantling a menu. 

So here's a bonus, let's say you are not feeling ethnic cuisine, and instead wind up in a steak house. We'll even here you can order food and not just a basic garden salad. (Something to remember; traditional meals consist of a protien, starch, and veggies.) so what I would do ( with out being "That" vegan) I'd order a side of items, but ask if it can be placed on a regular plate. So in the past ive done like some roasted veggies, a baked potato (kudos if the have yams or sweet potatoes) and some steamed white or brown rice, maybe some green beans or asparagus, do some steamed or Sautéd spinach. To this day i haven't found a steakhouse that doesn't serve these sides. 

Quick story:
Went on a date once to a spot my carnivorous (first red flag) friend recommended, turned out to be a heavy and I mean heavy meat spot that served all kinds of animals and sea food, luckily I called ahead and they accommodated me very well. Also was sure to make my dietary needs known by asking the server, turned out to be one of the better spots ive been to because they took food born illnesses and allergies seriously (seafood) and made sure not to share cooking utensils (meaning all my stuff was cooked in a pan instead of on a flat top.

text:
Here's what I'll say

There are a million good substitutions out there with massively varying price ranges. Very few of them taste just like the real thing if the taste of meat is what you like. You have to make a choice between your morals and something you think is delicious. Vegan food is just as good as food made with animal products, but it isn't always exactly the same.

I thought I could never give up dairy because butter and cheese were impossible to replace. I did it anyways because my ethics were more important than my pleasure. Now I have substitutions that fill the place dairy once did, my tastes just needed time to adjust.

My main tip is to eat meals that naturally contain no meat to start, don't try to replace it. Experiment with homemade seitan, tofu dishes, and beans! If you can't afford substitutes you don't have to use them but absolutely try them if they go on sale. Your tastes will adjust the longer you go without meat. If you really want this it'll be difficult, so always focus on your "why"

text:
ISO of more in-depth info on almonds.

Hi, all! I’ve been vegan for years and I am a regular volunteer at an animal sanctuary. Like everyone here, I’m really passionate about veganism. I’m also not afraid to speak up about it when challenged. I’ve gotten all sorts of edgy or idiotic comments over the years about veganism. Whether it’s people trying to discredit or make fun of my deeply held beliefs, invalidate the volunteer and activist work I do, etc. I thought I’d heard it all. But a friend’s new partner threw something at me a few weeks ago that I’d never dealt with before, and admittedly, it wasn’t something I was an expert on.

She said, “Almond trees are destroying the planet and therefore are hypocritical to vegan philosophy.” She then went on a rant about their water usage and pesticides. She said this at a restaurant immediately after I ordered my food and kindly asked the server to double-check with the kitchen that my dish could be made vegan. It’s very obvious she has some kind of attitude toward vegans, as she was very nice to me up until this point. I did a quick Google search and found countless articles backing up my point, two of which were peer-reviewed. I didn’t throw it in her face, but I did ask where she got that information from while showing her the facts I found from a Google search. She said she heard it from a friend, at which point I politely changed the subject. I saw her again at karaoke last week, and she brought it up AGAIN. I didn’t engage, as I’m not an environmental science expert and I just wanted to enjoy karaoke with friends. But I do want to learn as much about this topic as possible in the event she or anyone else comes at me with this again. She seems to feel pretty strongly about it.

I will eat organic beef, and organic pork but I will not eat organic poultry, I don’t think that is fair to them, and I do not eat fish.

If you are interested in more I wrote a book about the abuse I faced this year as a schizophrenic while living with a more ethical diet, from farm to slaughter.

text:
I’m not sure I can be of much help as I don’t have tips for how to get where I am, but I can tell you that you can get past this.  Here’s how I know….

I’m violently allergic to fish of any sort.  It’s not the usual “hives and itchy and can’t breathe” reaction.  It’s a violently throw up everything I’ve eaten for the last six days and then continue to be nauseated and sick for two or three more days. It’s bad. Really bad.

When I was growing up, my grandfather and my uncles and cousins were avid fishermen and my grandmother often had huge fish-fries with the results if their fishing activities.  Despite the fact that every time I ate fish, or even fries cooked in the same oil as the fish had been, I became violently ill and all the things I described above happened, every single time, I was STILL forced to eat enough fish, every time, to make that reaction happen.  I was told that I was “being dramatic” and I was such an ungrateful child that I was “willing myself” to be sick.  Yup.  It was first rate child abuse and it resulted in many years of trauma and psychotherapy later in life.

All that said, for many years my reaction to someone eating fish in my presence created similar reactions to what you’re describing. So much childhood trauma was wrapped up in everything having to do with cooking and eating fish, even the smell of it, was really difficult for me.

But…. At some point over the years (I’m 61 years old now), I was able to block out the upset and triggers that being near cooked fish, or worse, someone eating it, dredged up for me.  I’m still not totally okay with being near it, but it doesn’t cause the upset it used to.  I suspect you’ll eventually be in a similar place, though I have to admit, I don’t know how to get there. Just that it’s doable.  Somehow.  (I know, not real helpful but still…). 😊

text:
Ah thank you :)

Also my apologies for this wall of text.

All of ethics is about the question of how *should* we interact with the world. Should there be rules? rights? morals? I think we'd both say yes to that, but how can we figure out *what those morals should be*? For example, let's say all there is in the world is a rock by a creek. From looking at that rock and its surroundings, how can we determine how that rock *should be*? It's just a rock in a creek. There's nothing about it that tells us what it should be, it only tells us what it is.

Or let's say all there is is a poppy growing in a field, swaying in the wind. Should the poppy be swaying, or not swaying? If we could change something about this world, *should* we change something about it? How can we figure out what we *should* do to the world? We can do what we want to do, sure, but how can we figure out how the world *should be* based only on how it is? If all of these questions seem strange or meaningless, then you're on the right track.

This whole business of deciding how things *should be* is very difficult unless we pick some things to start with that we think should be. For example (to bring us back on topic), many vegans start with something along the lines of "we should avoid hurting beings that can feel pain". From there it is suddenly much easier to make further statements like "we shouldn't kill humans if we can avoid it" or "we shouldn't kill feeling animals if we can avoid it". But it's quite hard to say something like "we shouldn't hurt people" if you only have a rock and a creek, or a poppy in a field. We need to decide on something to start with that *isn't just how the world (or nature) already is*.

text:
Advice for Surviving a Renaissance Festival as a Vegan

This has probably been asked before (but I did actually search the subreddit before posting and didn't find anything relevant anywhere within the last five years really) but my vegan husband and I are being strong armed into going to the Renaissance festival closest to us (at Castle Gwynne in Tennessee) this Saturday by my in-laws (his parents). We're fairly newly married and this is the first big family event I've been invited to and most of hubby's immediate family will be going (siblings, nieces, nephews, etc.) and it will be the first time I'm getting to spend extended amounts of time with a lot of them, so while I could decline to go and just send hubby off to the wolves, I will be going. (Also husband is autistic and doesn't like big crowds and said he would be more comfortable with me there.)

I'm already having some ethical quandaries about going, but since father-in-law already paid for everyone's tickets and if we didn't go it would just be wasted money, I'm somewhat making peace with it to the extent that I can, but I know it will be an all-day affair, and while I'm fine surviving off of almost nothing due to being vegetarian and then vegan in the South for most of my life, my husband is still new (less than a full year fully vegan) and really easily disappointed by our food (and anything else) options when we go places, I was just wondering if anyone had any advice?

I looked up the venue and saw some of the vendors that will be there for the food options, but only one of them mentions having vegan breads and no other place mentions vegan food or seems to have a menu posted. I figure it'll be a find out when we get there situation, but that in itself is making me increasingly anxious. 

I love dressing up and playing make-believe, and my husband and I are really into DND and I used to LARP, so while I want to be excited for this excursion, I just feel sick and anxious. I also really don't want to upset our extended family on this very first outing I've been invited on. I've set pretty clear boundaries about my veganism with them so far, but I just don't want my husband to feel left out of rare family events this time or in the future if we don't go.

Any advice for compartmentalizing or surviving/scavenging for vegan options would be appreciated. I'm no stranger to asking a million questions about ingredients or materials, but I just want to hear others' experiences with this. 

Tldr: we have to go to the Ren fair, need tips on how to survive/keep newly vegan husband happy AND vegan over the course of a long day there.

Nietzsche and Veganism

Was learning about Friedrich Nietzsche today and got chatting with ChatGPT how this all relates to veganism. It came up with some cool content in the context of Nietzsche. 



A Will to Choose: A Vegan Manifesto Beyond Guilt

I do not abstain out of fear.
I choose out of power.
I see the violence of the world — and I do not look away.
I do not need illusions to shield me.
I face the blood, the hunger, the suffering — and I stand.

But I am not here to repeat nature.
I am here to surpass it.


---

I affirm life — not only mine, but all that pulses with it.
I do not follow appetite like a slave.
I reshape appetite like an artist.
I do not kill because I can.
I refrain because I choose — and that choice is strength.


---

My ethics are not inherited — they are forged.
Not dictated by pity.
Not commanded by dogma.
I do not act from guilt.
I act from vision.

If I say: “Let no scream go unheard,”
it is not because I am soft —
but because I am strong enough to live without causing them.


---

I am no slave — not to cruelty, not to guilt.
I am free.
And this freedom — I carve into my life.
Not to be righteous.
To be sovereign.

This is not the veganism of saints.
It is the veganism of creators.

text:
I think a vegan diet is a really shitty way to lose weight.

Most of the science-backed weight loss programs I've seen prioritize lean protein and veggies. It isn't that you *can't* get copious amounts of lean protein on a plant-based diet, but your choices are more limited (at least if you're working within the confines of what you're used to on the SAD), and a lot of our good sources of protein also come with bonus carbs. Carbs aren't evil - especially carbs that come with lots of fiber, like beans - but you end up eating more calories to hit that protein goal unless you're just pounding tofu and seitan. Generally I think protein is over-hyped in our diets, but it does seem to make a difference for weight loss. The show on Netflix *You Are What You Eat* sort of backed that theory to me; a lot of the participants assigned a vegan diet lost muscle as they lost weight (I don't think their meal plans placed any special focus on protein). 

I've fallen off every diet plan known to mankind. I am still vegan though, because veganism is an ethical stance that affects my dietary pattern, not a diet. It's just as easy for me to not eat hamburger as it is for me to not kick puppies. Those things are both unethical and I don't do them, end of story. If you want to convince yourself that eating animal products is unethical, watching Dominion is a great place to start. 

Being a vegan has not resulted in weight loss for me. Maybe it would for others, depending on their previous and new dietary patterns. If you're only going vegan for your own benefits, it's going to be like every other restrictive diet you've tried. You will probably lose weight as long as you stick to a WFPB diet, but if you can't stick to it - and boy I know how that works - then it's not going to give you the results you want.
label: arguments

text:
First, Humans are animals. Of course someone who happens to eat meat can have empathy for other humans, aka animals. It’s not accurate to claim that people who eat meat lack empathy for animals. Empathy isn't binary, it exists on a continuum and people express it in different ways, shaped by culture, experience, and personal reflection. Most humans do feel empathy for animals to at least some degree, it just isn't always extended equally to all species. In fact, humans, including meat eaters, often rescue injured wildlife, form deep emotional bonds with pets or donate to animal welfare causes. These acts clearly demonstrate concern and emotional connection.

What’s really happening is that many people operate with a kind of moral hierarchy, often placing humans at the top, followed by companion animals like dogs and cats, and then other animals based on perceived intelligence, emotional capacity, or relevance to human life. Some creatures, like parasites or insects, are often excluded from this circle of empathy entirely. This may not be consistent from a strictly utilitarian or rights-based ethical view, but it reflects how most people intuitively navigate moral questions. Empathy doesn’t disappear because someone eats meat. Instead, it’s filtered through cultural norms and a complex mix of values, some of which people may not have fully examined yet. If anything, conversations about diet and ethics should begin by recognizing that empathy is present, and then exploring how we might extend or refine it, rather than denying its existence outright.

text:
Hidden dangers of vegan food

Hello, some time ago I was on YouTube watching videos made by Violin MD, and in it she discussed a case of a woman who ate foods high in oxalate.

Because she was also eating a diet low in calcium she got a kidney condition called nephrocalcinosis (kind of like a kidney 
stone). 

I immediately began searching for more information about oxalate since I realized that the vegan foods I commonly consume are likely to have it.

https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7705561/

I relied on sesame seeds and chia to get calcium both of which are very high in oxalate. To make matters worse, this compound is not only damaging to the body, but also it diminishes the calcium that one can absorb. 

Because of this, I believe that vegans should also emphasize this when trying to convert people as this diet can have potential risks like; for example, a person not knowing they need to take vitamin B12 and they get a neurological condition. If people are affected by these conditions, this is likely to diminish the reputation of veganism. I read recently that a famous vegan quit because he got a "stone". 

https://www.businessinsider.com/liam-hemsworth-can-vegan-diet-cause-kidney-stones-2020-4

Now, I can understand to some degree why non vegans are so reluctant to change their diet. I believe this is something caused by natural selection as the human that strayed from their usual or "traditional diet" was likely to incur in danger from some unknown toxin. Traditional diets have techniques and special ways of preparing food to make it less dangerous such as fermentation or soaking tubers in milk. 

I'm not promoting for anyone to quit veganism. But I want vegans to be more aware of what they eat. So what do you think? Any other toxins lying in wait behind the bushes for the moment to strike?

text:
Give me one reason to abandon online vegan spaces.

Saw someone in a Discord server post news that a chicken factory burned down, killing thousands of chickens.

Someone responded that this was the most compassionate death they could have gotten.

I’m sorry, but I don’t think dying from inhaling flames and smoke while your feathers and flesh melt is “most compassionate.” And I think this is why vegan activism at every level matters.

To me, “most compassionate” would be a rescue and dying of age in as much comfort as possible. Even euthanasia would be more compassionate than burning alive. There are so many options besides gas chamber/beheading/burning alive.

I said my piece and then left the server. And leading up to now, I have been feeling very disillusioned with online vegan spaces.

I became vegan for the animals. I joined vegan spaces to find others who cared about animals. But there are far too many vegans who use online spaces to spew cruelty towards humans, and sometimes even animals. I think if we could hear one of those hens’ voices, she would wish to give that person a taste of what it’s like to burn to death in a cage, witnessing the screams and deaths of thousands of her sisters, and ask that person again if that is most compassionate (and at what point that person was planning on helping her).

None of it is compassionate.

The factory is not compassionate.

Burning is not compassionate.

Rescue and liberation is what is compassionate.

If I get an influx of downvotes on this because I actually give a damn what is good, I’m fucking abandoning vegan Reddit. Y’all can have your “compassion.” It disgusts me.

text:
My experience with Complement brand supplements

Hi all, I wanted to share my experience with Complement supplements. It's a brand marketed towards vegans and plant-based eaters, providing multivitamins, protein powder, etc. Overall, not a great experience, and I'll go into more detail below.

  
I ordered 3 things; the multivitamin, the protein powder, and creatine. The first order was the multivitamin and protein powder. The tracking they provided was for some third party service I never heard of called ACI Logistix and First Mile. It took a few days longer than the expected delivery, and the tracking didn't work until the day it was delivered. I live in the US, and barely ever have delivery issues. The multivitamins were fine, kinda hard to compare because they're like any other capsules. But the protein powder bag was torn open, so it was all over everything inside the box. I reached out to them, and they did generously replace the protein power even though I didn't ask for it. I told them that the free cup they provided seemed like is was broken, because the shaker thing inside didn't stay attached to the lid, making it kinda pointless. It's like a plastic half dome thing that is supposed to stay attached to the bottom of the lid, but it didn't stay attached. They also replaced that with another cup, but it had the exact same issue. So I had to conclude it's just a quality control issue with this kind of cup. The protein powder is alright, not the worst but not the best either.

  
The second order I got was the creatine. There was another issue with with the tracking, like the first package. It showed it wasn't active for the first week, and then second week went by and it was showing it had attempted delivery. But I was home, and nobody rang the buzzer. After I reached out and told them about it, they told me to wait a few more days, and they will finally refund my order. After a few more days go by (at this point it's been 3 weeks since ordering it), and the box finally arrives. This time, the bag had an even bigger tear, because the box was literally filled with white creatine powder with barely any left in the bag. The box itself was pretty beat up, so I'm guessing it's due to how this ACI Logistix and First Mile parcel service service treats their packages. 

  
It's kind of a shame that this service has such poor quality control. The poorly designed shaker cups aren't a huge deal, but flaky delivery that's way past the expected delivery date and damaged products are a bigger issue. I'd like to support vegan brands and services wherever I can, but this service sucks at making deliveries.
label: experience

Other People

Hi all, I would love to get some ideas/ insight on how to handle a particular issue, which is other people eating meat in front of you. I will be enjoying myself at a restaurant and then the server will come over to get our orders. Someone in my group orders a burger or steak, and suddenly I'm flooded with unease and discomfort. I try to be flexible. I'm okay if they order chicken, fish, even pork and grass-fed beef, but beef from a CAFO makes me so uncomfortable to hear people order (and watch them eat). I've just learned too much about how bad it is for the environment, let alone the animal cruelty. I feel like I'm complicit in the injustice by not speaking up.

I also think it's not too much to ask for my family to be aware of my discomfort and order *literally anything else* from the menu. There are usually so many options!

But now I'm in a quandary. Lately, l've been opening up more about my discomfort, when I would usually stay silent. I'm getting some mixed reactions. One particularly bad reaction was my sister-in-law, who told me l have no right to tell other people what to eat. I said it makes me feel nauseous to watch, and she said "take a Pepto Bismol."

I have no idea how to proceed. Should I just stop going to restaurants with her and the other family members who feel strongly about this? Or maybe make it clear I will only go if they offer grass-fed beef or don't have beef on the menu? I was even considering telling them that l'm going on strike against establishments that support CAFOs and suggest a list of restaurants that I'm not boycotting that we can all enjoy.

Have any of you struggled with this and come up with reasonable solutions? Or maybe you haven't and you can just relate? Any form of validation is welcome - I'm the only person I know who cares about this :( (I definitely want to start making more vegan/ vegetarian friends)

text:
Beyond Compassion: Do We Owe Animals More Than Just Care? An “Ethics of Discomfort” Perspective.

We often frame animal ethics around compassion and care, and that’s essential. But I wonder: **Is compassion enough? Or do we owe animals something even more demanding—our willingness to face the full discomfort of what our decisions mean for their lives?**

I’m working on a framework called the **“Ethics of Discomfort.”** Its core idea is that real responsibility doesn’t start with comfort but with the moments we’re forced to confront hard truths—about suffering, exploitation, and our complicity in them.

In animal ethics, this means:

* Not just *reducing* harm, but asking whether our entire societal structures should be transformed to remove animals from harm’s way altogether.
* Not hiding the realities of animal industries, but facing them directly—even when it’s emotionally painful.
* Accepting that our convenience and habits must be challenged, and that true change often starts in the discomfort zone.

**My question to this community:**  
How far should we, as a society, go to ensure that animals don’t merely survive under our protection—but that they are removed entirely from the sphere of human dominance and control?

Is veganism just a personal lifestyle choice, or should it be seen as a stepping stone towards a deeper ethical transformation of our social and political institutions?

There's nothing wrong with going vegan for someone else though. Aristotle believed that acquiring moral virtues through habituation was crucial for moral development. 

My ex gf went vegetarian for me (when I was still a vegetarian). Soon after we broke up for unrelated reasons, she joined an animal rights organization and went vegan about a year before I did.

My former roommate went vegan bc she didn't like cooking and I wouldn't prepare animal products for her. After a few months of eating only vegan food, she started to be disgusted by dead animals in food.

The main reason people  are aggressively dismissive about veganism is that they don't want to confront their own habits, much less change them. If you can get them to break these habits, they can take a step back and make a conscious decision much easier. 

The worst thing that could happen is that they refrain from eating animal products for a sorry time before they go back to their old habits. Even that would be better than nothing.

New to dating, and am struggling with non vegans

I’m new to dating, and it’s been a bit of a struggle so far. There’s not really any good places for vegans to connect. I know I’m not the only one in this boat, so I wanted to ask for some advice. 

It’s very important to me that my partner will have similar values as me, and veganism is a huge aspect of that. I’ve had so many great conversations with people, and then they bring up steaks or fishing or whatnot, and it’s just frustrating. I don’t know what to do or where to look.

text:
It's strange, I've been vegetarian and then vegan for a combined 40 years and I sometimes forget people eat the dead flesh of animals, not because I don't care about animals or I'm not aware of the process and it's atrocities but because it's just such a gross and foreign idea to me now.

When I meet someone and they start talking about a meat meal or even drinking dairy milk etc... I get this little shock wave of revulsion. Even if I like the person it's almost like they are contaminated, I can't imagine how they consume animals without thinking twice or questioning their choice, like it's so normal to them but so fundamentally abhorrent to me, it feels strange to exist in the same space.

Then I realize I'm the strange one and the odd one out in this scenario and my shock and revulsion is out of place and even anti social for pretty much everyone around me so I just notice it, suppress it and move on but it does affect how I feel about an individual once I know they've never even considered not eating animals.

The cancer element actually affects us all due to run off into water supplies and that's producing an increased risk of colon cancer in young people across the board whether you eat meat or not.

text:
Saw a totally normal TikTok and got upset :/

I saw a wellness TikTok today with a girl saying she’s trying to increase her vegetable and fiber intake. She said she had this “hack.” Which was following vegetarian or vegan recipes.

At first I was like, “oh it’s so cool that a new focus on fiber could show people that they can make vegan meals and that they are adequate.”

Then she straight up says, “I add meat because I’m not actually going to eat vegan or vegetarian. I need chicken. I need shrimp. I need beef.”

Maybe I’m just hormonal but I’ve never been so infuriated. I know it’s not fair for me to be mad, but I just felt disgust. You can’t even eat one meal without a corpse?

I’ve been feeling particularly angry lately. Ive had a couple panic attacks recently (not related to this, they are more spontaneous) I’ve felt like I was going to die each time. To feel that fear in my whole body, like inescapable doom, makes me feel worse for the animals. It devastates me.

Anyways, thanks for listening. I felt like I was making a mountain out of a molehill.
label: experience

I personally think the opposite. 

Diseases, heart diseases and cancers have increased drastically since we have adopted a more sugar and ultra processed based diet. 

The Inuit survived centuries living off animal and fat with minimal fruit or veg. They had drastically reduced numbers of the above diseases living primarily off of animal meats and fats. 

Since the dietary guidelines were introduced in the 80s which promotes a more carb/grain/fruit/veg lifestyle with minimal animal fats and meats, societies adopting these have gotten sicker and more obese. Research also shows that the majority of people are adopting these as they are integrated into care homes, schools, military installations and generally pushed by healthcare services. 

So I believe it’s either 1. The massive intervention of heave carb and sugar foods in our diets and/or 2. A lot of these heavy carb foods are laced with processed crap that our body doesn’t agree with.

Yep. Thank you so much for the thorough response!

Btw, we do fertilise meadows and pastures, and we do control weeds with herbicides in them where I live. That means heavy machinery and posions. Thus, crop deaths are involved even in mostly grass-fed cattle 😐. That's because the natural composition of a grassland doesn't always include the best species, nutritionally-wise, so pastures are still basically crops lands. This is clearly seen in countries where big ruminants weren't present prior to human settlement, e.g. New Zealand, where they had to establish completely non-native grassland in order to increase production. Plus it's often irrigated.

text:
No, veganism is not a diet

Veganism is not just a diet but a political and ethical movement rooted in anti-speciesism—the belief that humans are not superior to other animals. While eating vegan food occasionally may seem positive, it does not equate to embracing veganism, which involves a conscious rejection of all forms of animal exploitation in food, fashion, entertainment, and science. True veganism is a revolutionary stance aimed at animal liberation and has broader implications for health and the environment.

text:
Went on a date with a meat eater

Went on a date with a non vegan last night. It was cool for the most part, good vibes, laughs and got along. The only thing is I didn’t mention I’m a vegan. I am proud to be a vegan, but I feel like it’s stigmatized, so I usually keep it to myself. 

Anyways, I was texting them today and they said they went to a farmers market and got meat. I just got turned off pretty quickly. 

I went on this date, because I have been single for a very long time. I got tired of being lonely. I tried to meet other vegans at vegan events/meetups, Veggly, but had no luck. I downloaded a dating app and matched with this girl, but now I’m wondering if it’s a right fit. I don’t want someone to go vegan because of me, I want someone to go vegan because they feel it’s the right thing to do. 

Anyways, I don’t know what to do. Advice? I wish I could find a vegan partner. Can anyone relate?

text:
As a vegan, am I killing animals by not doing any vegan activism?

Imagine I live in a democratic country where an election is taking place. There are two parties: a "good" party and a "bad" party. I have the option to vote, and I know the "good party" is the better choice. But on election day, I choose not to vote, even though I know that if people like me don't vote, the bad party will likely win. As expected, the bad party comes to power.

Or simply just imagine someone knows that their house is burning, yet they choose to remain inactive. Why shouldn’t they be held responsible? Yes, they didn’t add fuel to the fire, but the fire kept spreading in the colony regardless. Many people suffered.

Now, apply this analogy to veganism. If I'm vegan but choose not to participate in vegan activism despite knowing that meat consumption is increasing day by day and that activism could help reduce animal suffering—am I, in some way, responsible for that harm, just like the person who didn’t vote and allowed the bad party to win? Am I letting non-vegans win?

[View Poll](https://www.reddit.com/poll/1kne36h)

Wanted to share tonight’s journal entry.. Started a regular gratitude practice, and this is what came up for me tonight— being vegan is pure JOY😌🌱

I’m grateful for finally deciding to take care of my health and for all the awesome ways that being vegan these past four months have surprisingly impacted my body (more energy, regular bowel movements, no more diarrhea or stomach aches, no more heaviness after eating, etc.). I wake up feeling refreshed and full of energy, and that’s something I’ve never experienced in my life. I went vegan for the animals and Mother Earth, but becoming healthier was the most awesome side effect I never expected. I no longer feel exhausted 24/7 and like my body is going to give out. I love the fact that I can actually physically feel my body getting healthier. It’s almost euphoric how much I love it.
